content.text: React exposes two overlapping families of events for tracking cursor and touch input: the classic mouse event family (onClick, onMouseEnter, …) and the newer pointer event family (onPointerDown, onPointerMove, …). Mouse events were designed for desktop cursors; pointer events cover mouse, touch, and stylus with a single unified API. For anything that must work across devices — drag, draw, long-press — prefer pointer events.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison of mouse events (onClick, onMouseEnter/Leave, onMouseOver/Out, onMouseDown/Up, onMouseMove) versus pointer events (onPointerDown/Up, onPointerMove, onPointerEnter/Leave, onPointerCancel) showing which events bubble and which are desktop-only
attrs.title: Mouse Events vs Pointer Events in React

content.text: Every mouse event handler receives a
(React's SyntheticEvent wrapping the browser's native MouseEvent). The most commonly used props are:
Cursor position relative to the viewport
Position relative to the page (includes scroll)
Position relative to the element's padding edge
Which buttons are currently held (for drag)
Modifier keys held at time of click
The DOM element the event fired on
Where the cursor came from (mouseenter) or went to (mouseleave)
The most common event. React fires
only for the left mouse button release (button 0). It also fires for touch taps on mobile. Use the
property when you need to detect right or middle clicks — but use
is only triggered by the left button.
Do not put a double-click handler on the same element as a single-click handler unless you intentionally want both to fire — a double-click always triggers two
. Debouncing or tracking the first click in state is the usual workaround.
This is the most common source of hover-related bugs. The distinction:
— fire once when the cursor crosses the element's boundary. They do NOT bubble. Use these for simple hover state on a single element.
— bubble up from child elements. Moving the cursor from a child to a parent fires
on the parent — and vice versa. The parent's handler fires even when the cursor is inside a nested child.
onMouseMove and Drag with Mouse Events
Mouse move fires continuously as the cursor moves over the element. Attach it to the document (via a ref or
) when dragging, so movement outside the element is still tracked. A mouse drag in pure React mouse events looks like this:
Pointer events unify mouse, touch, and pen input under one set of props. React exposes
. They fire for all pointer types:
The PointerEvent object adds several fields on top of the standard MouseEvent properties:
— unique ID for this pointer. Multiple simultaneous touches get different IDs.
. Use this to branch on device type.
— 0–1 float; how hard the user is pressing (stylus / some touchscreens).
— contact area in CSS pixels (useful for touch).
— true for the first finger in a multi-touch gesture.
The major advantage of pointer events over mouse events for drag interactions is
routes all subsequent pointer events to that element, even if the pointer moves off it entirely. This eliminates the need for global window listeners:
— always set this CSS property on a draggable element that handles pointer events. Without it the browser may intercept the touch to scroll the page before your handler sees it.
Unlike mouse events, a pointer can disappear without an
. Common causes: the user takes an incoming phone call, the browser scrolls and takes over the touch, or the stylus leaves the digitizer range.
A common UI pattern: show a tooltip when hovering an element.
are the right events because they fire exactly once at the outer boundary.
For effects like custom cursors or parallax, you track the cursor position continuously. Because
fires many times per second, avoid calling
on every event if the update doesn't need to trigger a re-render — use a ref and update the DOM directly.
When to Use Mouse Events vs Pointer Events
Works on touch too; built-in for keyboard activation
Works on touch and pen; capture avoids window listeners
Fires for right-click AND long-press on mobile
Desktop-only feature anyway; mouse coords are what you need
— fires repeatedly when the cursor enters child elements, causing flicker. Use
Forgetting touchAction: 'none' on pointer drag targets
— the browser will scroll instead of letting your handler track the pointer.
Using window listeners for drag instead of setPointerCapture
— more code, less reliable. Pointer capture is the correct primitive.
Calling setState on every onMouseMove event
— 60+ re-renders per second. Use a ref + direct DOM manipulation or throttle with
when the value drives visual-only updates.
Expecting mouse events on touch devices
— mobile browsers fire mouse events as a compatibility shim but with a 300ms delay. For anything interactive, use pointer events or React's

React exposes both the classic mouse event family and the modern pointer event family. Prefer pointer events for anything that must work on touch or stylus.
for hover state — they don't bubble and fire once at the element boundary.
in drag interactions so pointer events remain routed to the dragged element even when the pointer leaves it.
on elements that handle pointer events for drag — otherwise the browser steals the touch for scrolling.
in drawing and drag components to clean up state when the pointer is unexpectedly lost.
updates that only affect the DOM visually, update a ref and write to the DOM directly rather than calling setState on every event.
